stem: A biology class is studying how genetic information in DNA is used to build proteins. The teacher provides data on a short segment of a gene and the molecules produced from it in a healthy cell.

stimulus.title: Molecules produced from a gene segment
stimulus.tableMarkdown:
| Stage | Molecule | Location in cell |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | DNA template strand | Nucleus |
| 2 | mRNA transcript | Nucleus to cytoplasm |
| 3 | Polypeptide (protein) | Ribosome |

parts.label: A
parts.prompt: Identify the molecule in the table that carries the genetic code from the nucleus to the ribosome.
parts.scoringGuidance: Full credit: names mRNA (messenger RNA) as the molecule that carries the code to the ribosome.

parts.label: B
parts.prompt: Explain how the sequence of bases in the mRNA determines the sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide.
parts.scoringGuidance: Full credit: explains that ribosomes read mRNA in three-base codons and each codon specifies one amino acid, so the base order sets the amino acid order.

parts.label: C
parts.prompt: Predict how a change in one DNA base could affect the final protein, and justify your prediction.
parts.scoringGuidance: Full credit: predicts a possible change to a codon that could change (or not change) an amino acid, and connects that to a possible change in the protein's shape or function.

criteria.0: No parts answered correctly, or responses show no understanding of how DNA directs protein synthesis.
criteria.1: One part answered correctly; limited understanding of the DNA-to-protein pathway.
criteria.2: Two parts answered correctly; partial understanding of how base sequence determines protein structure.
criteria.3: All three parts answered correctly; clear understanding of transcription, translation, and the effect of mutations.

keyTerms.definition: A single-stranded RNA molecule that carries the genetic code from DNA to the ribosome.
A sequence of three mRNA bases that specifies one amino acid.
The process in which ribosomes read mRNA codons to build a polypeptide.

annotatedResponses.response: Part A: DNA. Part B: The protein just forms. Part C: Nothing happens.
annotatedResponses.annotation: No part is correct; the response does not identify mRNA or describe the codon-to-amino-acid relationship.

annotatedResponses.response: Part A: mRNA carries the code to the ribosome. Part B: The bases make the protein. Part C: The protein changes.
annotatedResponses.annotation: Only Part A is correct; Parts B and C lack the codon mechanism and a justified prediction.

annotatedResponses.response: Part A: mRNA. Part B: Ribosomes read mRNA in codons of three bases and each codon codes for one amino acid, so the base order sets the amino acid order. Part C: The protein might change.
annotatedResponses.annotation: Parts A and B are correct; Part C states a possible change but gives no justification connecting it to protein shape or function.

annotatedResponses.response: Part A: mRNA carries the genetic code from the nucleus to the ribosome. Part B: The ribosome reads the mRNA in three-base codons, and each codon specifies one amino acid, so the order of bases determines the order of amino acids in the polypeptide. Part C: If one DNA base changes, the matching mRNA codon can change and specify a different amino acid; that new amino acid could alter the protein's three-dimensional shape and change or disrupt its function.
annotatedResponses.annotation: All three parts are correct: mRNA is identified, the codon-to-amino-acid mechanism is explained, and the mutation prediction is justified through protein structure and function.

blueprint.stemAffordance: A table listing the molecules produced as a gene is expressed, giving concrete referents for each part.
blueprint.compatibilityRationale: All three KCs sit on the central-dogma pathway and share the single gene-expression scenario.
blueprint.cognitiveDemand: Progresses from identification to mechanism to justified prediction.

A.function: Identify the information-carrying molecule from the stimulus.

C.function: Predict and justify the effect of a mutation.

blueprint.evidencePattern: Students cite rows of the table to ground their answers.
expectedResponseElements: mRNA named as the carrier molecule
codons of three bases each specifying one amino acid
a justified prediction linking a base change to protein function
commonIncompleteResponses: naming DNA instead of mRNA in Part A
omitting the three-base codon detail in Part B
predicting a change in Part C without justifying it
